title: Information Aggregation via Contracting
abstract: When a group of investors with dispersed private information jointly invest in a risky project, how should they divide the project's profit? We show that a simple contract dividing profits in proportion to investors' risk tolerances may facilitate information aggregation by altering investors' risk-taking incentives when they decide on how investment strategies respond to private information. Our results provide a contracting-based approach for information aggregation, which is an alternative to learning from endogenous market variables (e.g., prices) via contingent schedules as seen in well-known rational expectations equilibrium models.

article.introduction:
A large literature studies informationaggregation via secondary market trading. In these papers, investors submit sets of limit orders that constitute demand schedules for a risky asset in inelastic supply, and equilibrium trading quantities are contingent on the realized market-clearing price, which is an implicit function of all investors' information.1In contrast, in this paper, we study the less explored topic of information aggregation viacontracting. Besides being of intellectual interest, there are economic motivations for such a focus. For example, many general finance settings such as primary markets may not feature endogenous market-clearing prices or may lack infrastructures similar to limit order books to facilitate information aggregation.
  Intuitively, in a setting beyond secondary market trading, investors may choose equilibrium actions contingent on others' realized actions, as they are also implicit functions of others' information. For example, when a group of investors with dispersed private information fund a risky production technology of elastic size, they may choose individual investment amounts conditional on all investors' total investment amounts. This paper shows that a well-designed contract that divides profits appropriately among all investors may substitute such contingent schedules for information aggregation, leading to an informationally efficient allocation.
  SectionIpresents the main results of our paper. We focus on the problem of investing in a project with a constant-return-to-scale (CRS) production technology. This case is of particular interest for at least three reasons, as I will discuss in more detail later. First, information aggregation is unambiguously desirable with CRS projects, for which informational efficiency leads to Pareto efficiency. Second, CRS projects' production technologies stand in sharp contrast to, and thus complement, familiar rational expectations equilibrium (REE) models on how the market aggregates information. Finally, that individual investment amounts do not impose externalities on the project return relieves a contract of other confounding roles such as incentive alignment and allows us to most cleanly demonstrate a contract's power in information aggregation.
  A somewhat counterintuitive implication of our analysis is that a contract that facilitates information aggregation may have each investor agree ex ante to a share of the project's profit not in proportion to their initial investment amount. An illustration is provided in SectionI.C: In a simple two-agent example with constant-relative-risk-averse (CARA) preferences and normally-distribited risks/signals will show that two identically risk-averse but heterogeneously informed investors would be better off under a 50-50 profit split, even if their investment amounts differ because under this contract both investors obtain profits equal to what they would have received had they been fully informed about each other's private information and hence made the most informative investment decisions. This example will also shed light on the intuition behind our main result in Proposition1, which allows for an arbitrary number of investors of heterogeneous risk aversion and presents conditions under which contracts that divide profits in proportion to risk tolerances facilitate perfect information aggregation. The rest of SectionIfurther discusses implementation details such as contract parameter solicitation and limited liability protection.
  To better understand the striking main results of SectionI, in SectionII, we compare CRS setups to the more familiar REE models that also emphasize information aggregation. SectionII.Aassumes that no additional contract is imposed and draws an analogy between price-contingent demand schedules in an REE model and total dollar investment–contingent schedules that constitute a Bayesian Nash equilibrium. We show that both schedules facilitate information aggregation despite operating under different production technologies, with REE models assuming fixed quantities and flexible prices, while CRS setups assume fixed prices and flexible quantities. SectionII.Bthen clarifies the potential role of contracting in both settings, showing that a well-designed contract may substitute learning from endogenous variables using contingent schedules and induce informationally efficient allocations.
  Although contingent schedules and contracting may induce the same informationally efficientallocation, they do so via different means and induce different equilibriumstrategies. Specifically, contingent schedules help investors learn others' information from endogenous information-aggregating variables and induce equilibrium strategies identical to those when all investors are perfectly informed about each other's information. In contrast, a well-designed contract changes risk-taking incentives, such that it induces equilibrium strategies that reflect one's own information only. The latter strategies typically differ from those in which all investors are perfectly informed. Nevertheless, together with the contract that redistributes profits, such strategies may still lead to the same informationally efficient allocation.
  In SectionIII, we study the Pareto efficiency of the proposed contract. We show that thanks to the absence of externalities, informational efficiency and Pareto optimality coincide in our baseline model with CRS production technologies. The informationally efficient allocation under a well-designed contract is therefore also Pareto efficient. We also explore non-CRS scenarios and use an example to highlight a key insight: In the presence of externalities, the equivalence between informational and Pareto efficiency generally no longer holds, but contracts may still induce Pareto optimal allocations by both aggregating information as shown for CRS projects and additionally by aligning incentives so that investors internalize their externalities on others. We note that the former is a novel insight of this paper, while the latter is more common in the literature.
  SectionIVconcludes and discusses potential implications of the theoretical results, both for the financial market and for business activities under decentralized information. We focus on offering fresh perspectives rather than on matching every observed practice.

  The intuition for Corollary1is that as information asymmetry vanishes, the proportions of investment amounts approach the proportions of risk tolerances. An implication of Corollary1is that the familiar practice of dividing revenue in proportion to initial investment amounts is optimal within our framework as long as information asymmetry is absent.
  We discuss two practicalities of implementation.
  First, as our proposed profit-sharing contract requires knowledge of everyone's share of aggregate risk tolerances, it has to be implemented in multiple steps: (1) investors report personal information to help gauge their risk aversions, upon which the contract is drawn; (2) a new project arrives and investors decide how much to invest based on both private signals and the agreed-upon contract; and (3) the realization ofrand the resulting total project payoff is divided according to the contract in place. A natural concern that then arises is whether investors have incentives to misreport in step (1). Proposition2shows that this is not the case.

article.II. Contract versus Market for Informati:
Information aggregation, the key advantage of the profit-sharing contract for investors facing a CRS project, is also central to REE models of the financial market, in which prices aggregate information and investors learn from price-contingent demand schedules. Hence, exploring the relation between two forms of information aggregation—contracting as presented in this paper, and learning with contingent schedules, as presented in the previous literature, could help us better appreciate the results in SectionI. Accordingly, in SectionII.A, we first discuss the similarities and differences between the CRS and REE setup. In SectionII.B, we then clarify the roles that the profit-sharing contract could play in both settings. The main takeaway from this section is that contracting and learning with contingent schedules may be substitutes for information aggregation.
  In this section, we first compare and contrast our CRS setup and an REE model assuming no contract in place, highlighting their differences and similarities. We show that the major difference between the two setups lies in their underlying production technologies. Despite this difference, the two setups share a similarity in that they can both obtain information aggregation by allowing investors to submit investment schedules conditional on endogenous variables. In an REE setup, the endogenous variable takes the familiar form of the market-clearing price, and investment schedules are the familiar price-contingent demand schedules of asset shares. In our CRS setup, the endogenous variable takes the form of the sum of all investors' equilibrium dollar investment amounts, and investment schedules can be total dollar amount–contingent investment strategies. These results set the stage for SectionII.B, which reintroduces contracts to both settings and further shows how contracts may substitute the aforementioned learning from endogenous variables to also facilitate information aggregation.
  This section reviews the familiar result in existing literature on how price aggregates information in an REE, so that the next section can develop analogous concepts in a CRS setup for comparison. Recall the familiar CARA-normal REE model à la Grossman (1976). article.III. Pareto Efficiency:
Throughout the paper so far, we have focused on informational efficiency, that is, on how the profit-sharing contract may substitute contingent schedules for information aggregation, so that each investor gets the same profit as when perfectly informed about all investors' private signals. A natural question that then arises is whether such informationally efficient allocations are also Pareto efficient. This section explores this question. Specifically, to offer a unified treatment for both CRS and non-CRS production technologies, SectionIII.Abegins by defining and characterizing Pareto optimality among investors facing a project with a general (not necessarily CRS) production technology. SectionIII.Bthen presents our main takeaway: In the CRS setup of SectionI, thanks to no externality, the informationally efficient allocation induced by the profit-sharing contract as in Proposition1(or by contingent schedules as in Proposition3) is indeed Pareto optimal. To shed light on the importance of the CRS assumption for this result, SectionIII.Cpresents an example without the CRS assumption and shows that the equivalence between informational and Pareto efficiency may no longer hold. The section then discusses the multiple roles contracts may play in non-CRS setups, besides substituting contingent schedules for information aggregation.
  For a unified treatment of both CRS and potentially non-CRS setups, we assume that investors face a general production technology characterized by revenue functionπ(r,Y)$\pi (r, Y)$, whereY=∑i=1nyi$Y = \sum _{i=1}^n y_i$again sums all investors' dollar investments. As we explain earlier, for the CRS setup in SectionIthat we are most interested in,π(r,Y)=(1+r)Y$\pi (r, Y) = (1+r) Y$.
  Following Holmström and Myerson (1983), we define Pareto optimality over a planner's (dynamic)decision rules, which consists of two steps. First, the planner decides how much money all investors should invest in the project. Second, the planner stipulates how much each investor should get back when the project pays off. In the first step, the planner cannot know the ex post realization ofr, so he chooses a total investment amountY(s)$Y(s)$as a function of the entire signal profilesacross all investors. By the second step, however,rwould have realized, so the planner stipulates how much each investor gets back,{qi(r,Y)}i=1n$\lbrace q_i(r, Y)\rbrace _{i=1}^n$, as a function of both theYchosen in the first step and the ex post realizationr.

  Pareto optimality as given by Definition6is in an “ex ante” sense because it measures investors' welfare byunconditionalexpected utilities, rather than, say, expected utilities conditional on one's own private signalsi$s_i$, all investors' signal profiles, or the ex post realization ofr. By the law of iterated expectations, ex ante Pareto optimality implies other definitions of Pareto optimality. Definition6therefore presents the strongest notion of Pareto efficiency and describes what a planner can choose within physical constraints so that no investor can be better off without making at least one investor worse off.

Specifically, when Pareto weights areai∝ρi−1$a_i \propto \rho _i^{-1}$, the lump-sum transfers equal zero, and profits are divided in proportion to risk tolerances, as prescribed by the profit-sharing contract.
  For CRS projects,π(r,Y)=(1+r)Y$\pi (r,Y) = (1 + r) Y$. We can thus explicitly solve forY∗(s)$Y^*(s)$from (12), which together with Lemma2shows that the profit-sharing contract in SectionIinduces a Pareto optimal allocation as an equilibrium outcome, as summarized next.

  Recall that Proposition1in SectionIhas shown that in a CRS setup, the profit-sharing contract induces an informationally efficient outcome. Proposition5further shows that this informationally efficient outcome is also Pareto efficient. This finding is not too surprising—in the CRS setup investors do not compete with, and thus impose no externalities on, each other, so an informationally efficient outcome will always be Pareto efficient as well.
  When we go beyond the CRS setup, this equivalence between informational and Pareto efficiency may no longer hold. This is because, unlike in the CRS setup where investors do not directly “compete” with each other, their actions toward a non-CRS project may also impose externalities on each other; if investors do not fully internalize such externalities, the resulting equilibrium allocation will then generally be Pareto suboptimal, even if information aggregation is perfect. That said, a well-designed contract may still preserve Pareto optimality by facilitating information aggregation and aligning incentives. The next section illustrates this point with a CARA-normal example.

  In the default case of no contract in place, such that investors divide the project's revenue in proportion to initial investments rather than divide the project's profit in proportion to risk tolerances, an informationally efficient allocation can be characterized by having all investors know the entire signal profiles.
  Corollary 3.For a project withπ(r,Y)≡(1+r−λY)Y$\pi (r,Y) \equiv (1+r-\lambda Y) Y$, wherer∼∼N(r¯,τr−1)$\tilde{r}\sim \mathcal {N}(\bar{r}, \tau _r^{-1})$,si=r+εi$s_i = r + \epsilon _i$,εi∼N(0,τi−1)$\epsilon _i\sim \mathcal {N}(0,\tau _i^{-1})$, andλ≥0$\lambda \ge 0$, if all investors know the entire signal profiles, then their total investment under no contract in place isY(s)=τrr¯+∑j=1nτjsj(1+∑j=1nλ(τr+∑j=1nτj)ρj+λ(τr+∑j=1nτj))∑j=1n(ρj+λ(τr+∑j=1nτj))−1$Y(s) = \frac{\tau _r \bar{r} + \sum _{j=1}^n \tau _j s_j}{ (1 + \sum _{j=1}^n \frac{\lambda (\tau _r + \sum _{j=1}^n \tau _j)}{\rho _j + \lambda (\tau _r + \sum _{j=1}^n \tau _j)}) } \sum _{j=1}^n (\rho _j + \lambda (\tau _r + \sum _{j=1}^n \tau _j) )^{-1}$.
  
  Comparison of Corollary2and Corollary3shows that whenλ>0$\lambda >0$, the informationally efficient allocation under no contract in place will differ from a Pareto optimal allocation. The difference results from the externalities associated with the decreasing-return-to-scale production technology: More investment from a single investor tends to lower the per-dollar return to everyone else. Because investors do not internalize such externalities, they tend to “overuse” their private information and thus compete away the value of their information. The fact that this difference disappears whenλ=0$\lambda =0$, that is, in a CRS setup with no externality, further confirms that it is the presence of externalities that leads to the wedge between informational efficiency and Pareto efficiency when we move beyond the CRS assumption.
  In settings with both dispersed information and externalities, a contract may have two roles: In addition to substituting contingent schedules for information aggregation, as we emphasize in this paper, a contract may also align incentives to mitigate distortions from externalities, as is well recognized in textbook discussions on taxes. Indeed, in our illustrative example, a contract that divides project profits in proportion to risk tolerances continues to induce a Pareto optimal allocation even if externalities exist (whenλ>0$\lambda >0$), as the next proposition shows.
  Proposition 6.For a project withπ(r,Y)≡(1+r−λY)Y$\pi (r,Y) \equiv (1+r-\lambda Y) Y$, wherer∼∼N(r¯,τr−1)$\tilde{r}\sim \mathcal {N}(\bar{r}, \tau _r^{-1})$,si=r+εi$s_i = r + \epsilon _i$,εi∼N(0,τi−1)$\epsilon _i\sim \mathcal {N}(0,\tau _i^{-1})$, andλ≥0$\lambda \ge 0$, a contract that pays investoriher initial investment plusκi=ρi−1∑j=1nρj−1$\kappa _i=\frac{\rho _i^{-1}}{\sum _{j=1}^n \rho _j^{-1}}$of the project's net profit induces a Pareto optimal Bayesian Nash equilibrium.
  
  The case ofλ=0$\lambda =0$corresponds to a CRS production technology. In this case, Proposition6is immediate from Proposition5, which states that for a CRS project, an informationally efficient allocation induced by the profit-sharing contract (Proposition1) is also Pareto optimal.
  The case ofλ>0$\lambda >0$corresponds to a decreasing-return-to-scale production technology. Although earlier results show that in this case, the presence of externalities may lead to differences between a purely informationally efficient allocation and a Pareto efficient allocation, Proposition6shows that the equilibrium under contracting may nevertheless still be Pareto optimal. This is because in addition to facilitating information aggregation, a contract that bonds investors together can also help align incentives, prevent excessive competition, and thus preserve the overall value of their information.21
  Since a contract's role in overcoming externalities goes beyond information aggregation, our main results on information aggregation (e.g., the baseline model in SectionI) focus on the CRS case to most cleanly demonstrate the power of contracts for information aggregation without confounding forces.
article.IV. Conclusion:
In this paper, we show that contracts may provide a useful alternative for information aggregation. We first demonstrate the power of information aggregation via contracting by showing that in a stylized CRS investment setting, in which informational efficiency and Pareto efficiency coincide, a simple contract that divides total profits in proportion to investors' risk tolerances returns to investors the same amount as an informationally efficient benchmark would deliver. We then show that our contract may substitute contingent schedules on endogenous variables, such as price-contingent demand schedules in standard REE models, which have typically been applied to describe how markets aggregate information. We finally show that in the absence of externalities, the informationally efficient allocation induced by the profit-sharing contract is also Pareto optimal, while when externalities are present, the profit-sharing contract may additionally align incentives and coordinate the use of information to obtain Pareto optimal allocations.
  While our contributions are mainly theoretical, we conclude the paper with a brief note on potential applications. Our goal is not to match every empirical pattern, but rather to offer some fresh perspectives based on theory. First, since our model corresponds to a setting in which a group of investors with dispersed private information jointly invest in a risky project/asset, our results may have implications for the financial market. Specifically, just as REE models study information aggregation via price-contingent limit order schedules in secondary markets, our finding on contracts substituting contingent schedules may provide an alternative approach for aggregating information in primary markets, which typically lack well-established limit order books to enable submissions of contingent schedules.22
  Second, if we replace capital investment with production inputs and the random return with stochastic factor productivity, our proposed contract is also similar to a partnership structure. To see this, note that a partnership firm is typically defined by a charter among multiple partners that stipulates ex ante how to divide any ex post profits earned by the firm. After a partnership is formed, business operations start, and the firm's profit may see random shocks that could be perceived as stochastic factor productivity. Each partner brings certain production inputs to the firm and uses information about the factor productivity to decide how much to contribute. Remuneration typically consists of two parts: direct compensation for productive input contribution and a proportion of the firm's residual earnings as determined by the charter. Examples include law firms in which partners contribute hours (as production inputs) and receive hourly bills (as direct compensation) as well as profit shares, or some farmers' cooperatives that resemble partnerships among their members. In both our contract setting and a partnership, remuneration consists of two parts: the returned initial investment, analogous to direct compensation for production input contribution, and a share of the project's net profit, analogous to a share in the partnership's residual earnings. This connection suggests that information aggregation may be one of the rationales behind some partnership structures.
  While this paper shows that contracts may facilitate information aggregation, we merely scratch the surface here, leaving unanswered many follow-up questions such as the interactions between contracts and contingent schedules in more general setups and their resulting informational and Pareto efficiency properties. We nevertheless hope our results offer fresh perspectives for topics such as investment, rational expectations, and the theory of the firm, as well as inspire future research.
